data.title: Disparate Property Supply In Tampa Creates Selective Opportunity
data.description:
The old real estate adage Location, Location, Location is as relevant today as ever, but I would like to amend the 3 pillars to Location, Supply, and Demand.

Tampa has become one of the premier real estate locations, but it also serves as perfect evidence that supply and demand nuances are just as important as location.

Let me begin by discussing the dynamics that make Tampa a generally great real estate market and then show how one can use supply and demand specifics to home in on favored property types and the specific companies that own those properties.

Tampa – strong job and population growth

People like nice weather and favorable taxes. Florida has become a beacon for those seeking such conditions. Newsweek reports on its population growth soaring past that of the rest of the Nation.

“Florida's population has also grown since before the pandemic: according to Census Bureau data, the Sunshine State counted a total of 21,538,192 residents on April 1 2020—by July 1, 2024, this number had gone up to 23,372,215”

Tampa specifically is expected to continue growing, with Claritas estimates calling for another 5.7% population growth by 2030.

Household income in Tampa is expected to grow an impressive 12.13%.

More people and more income is a great baseline for basically every kind of real estate. That makes Tampa a generally strong Location.

However, with the disparate property performances shown below, it is clear that location alone is not enough.

The latest Case Shiller report reveals Tampa as the single worst MSA for home appreciation, coming in at -1.51%, while home prices at a national level increased by 4.08%

At the same time, Tampa retail real estate is performing great. Marcus and Millichap ranks Tampa as the 3rd best MSA for retail. Rents on shopping centers are forecast to tick up 5.1% in 2025.

The location is the same, so what is the difference?

The difference is the other pillars of supply and demand.

Housing has had substantial construction in Tampa in the form of both apartments and single-family homes. This additional supply dilutes the value of standing real estate by increasing competition.

On the single-family home side, the supply is also increased by new listings of existing homes. Newsweek discusses a surge in listings.

“Compared to January 2024, when the state counted a total of 118,163 home listings, active inventory for sale was up 33 percent.”

Home insurance and HOA fees have risen dramatically throughout Florida, which is hurting homeowners and encouraging some to sell. With so many homes being marketed to potential home buyers, sellers are having to lower their prices to get their homes sold.

In contrast, retail has had very little new supply. Completions of new retail real estate have consistently been at less than 1% of existing inventory, which is barely even enough to keep up with demolitions of the old age of the property.

With the paucity of new construction, incremental demand has outpaced supply and the retail vacancy rate has dropped to about 3% (white line on top graph). Retailers are now competing for space in landlord’s properties, which has allowed landlords to charge higher rent. Asking rents have risen to almost $25 per foot from below $15 just 10 years ago.

Further rent increases are expected as retail space remains scarce.

Tampa remains a great location. Its job and population growth fuel demand for all kinds of real estate, but the way that actually hits property fundamentals is altered by supply dynamics.

The high supply of housing to the submarket has overwhelmed demand, causing a temporary downturn.

The low supply of retail in combination with demand growth has created a scarcity of real estate and made the market highly landlord-favored.

Thus, when we look beyond location to the other pillars of real estate fundamentals, we want to invest in Tampa shopping centers and avoid Tampa single-family homes. To actually implement this into investment we turn to the Portfolio Income Solutions Property Directory, which is available to our subscribers. In this large spreadsheet, we document the holdings of each REIT and each property type within each of the top 50 submarkets.

Here are the REITs that own shopping centers in Tampa.

I am not a fan of Phillips Edison & Company, Inc. (PECO) due to valuation, but Kimco Realty Corporation (KIM), Brixmor Property Group Inc. (BRX), Kite Realty Group Trust (KRG), Saul Centers, Inc. (BFS) and Slate Grocery REIT (OTC:SRRTF) are each well positioned right now.

Similarly, one might want to avoid single-family homes in Tampa. Here is the list of REIT owners in the MSA.

These are fine companies, and they of course own property in many markets. I don’t think Tampa's weakness alone makes them uninvestable. It is merely something to consider as part of an overall investment thesis.

Invitation Homes Inc. (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) are trading on the pricier side of REITs, so I don’t find either particularly opportunistic at the moment. Tricon was bought out by Blackstone Inc. (BX) making it no longer tradable as a standalone entity.

Other markets

Examining the 3 pillars of real estate fundamentals (location, supply, demand) reveals opportunities in various submarkets and various property types.

Phoenix has similar dynamics to Tampa with high population growth combined with heavy construction of housing and low construction of shopping centers. Whitestone REIT (WSR) is particularly well positioned there as it benefits from the population and job growth while not having to fight a competing new supply of shopping centers.

Apartments are heavily supplied across much of the U.S. right now, but certain Midwestern markets received far less construction volume, making Midwestern apartment REITs like Centerspace (CSR) opportunistic.

Construction of new energy assets is gummed up by a multi-year regulatory review process which makes existing utility infrastructure invaluable in areas with spiking electricity demand. Virginia, Arizona, and Georgia are epicenters of data center growth, where a large portion of the existing generation is owned by Dominion Energy, Inc. (D), Pinnacle West Capital Corporation (PNW), and The Southern Company (SO), respectively.

The bottom line

Location is a key consideration for real estate, but it is really unlocked by also considering the supply and demand fundamentals specific to a given location.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Diverging Paths: A Look At WisdomTree's Best And Worst ETFs In Q1 2025
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The Top Five ETFs

Gold and Gold Mining Equities

The Fund at the top of the leaderboard—the WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN)1—didn't just outperform. It broke the mold. Most investors still view gold as an inert hedge, something you own when you fear the world is about to come undone. But in the first three months of 2025, gold surged for reasons far more nuanced: central banks accumulated reserves at a brisk pace2; geopolitics offered little reassurance; and inflation, though retreating, showed hints of embedded persistence.3 What made GDMN exceptional wasn't just its exposure to gold—it was the capital-efficient structure that overlaid gold futures onto an exposure to gold mining stocks.4 In an environment where both bullion and gold mining stocks moved higher, GDMN was strong on both components of this trade. Most investors weren't looking for gold to outperform equity markets, but it did. We also remind investors that gold miners tend to represent a leveraged exposure to the price of gold, meaning if the price of gold is rising, mining stocks can deliver an even larger response. Of course, this can also work in opposite fashion if gold's price falls. In this period, GDMN's exposure to gold miners AND gold worked beautifully in the positive direction.

A Q1 2025 Surprise—Chinese Equities

Just below it on the performance tables was the WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned-Enterprises Fund (CXSE),5 a Fund that cuts a deliberate path through the complexity of Chinese equities. In 2025, the first thought of most U.S. investors on China is likely about the intense, ongoing geopolitical rivalry. Headlines generated by the Trump Administration or other politicians definitely capture an adversarial tone. Yet, in the face of that noise, Chinese technology-focused equities6 delivered a strong Q1 2025, at least viewed relative to other global equity markets, and CXSE's exposure tilted more in this direction than toward other sectors.

Developed International Value Strategies

Meanwhile, a quiet but important theme re-emerged: developed international dividends and the developed international value style got interesting again. The WisdomTree International High Dividend Fund (DTH)7, the WisdomTree International AI Enhanced Value Fund (AIVI)8 and the WisdomTree International LargeCap Dividend Fund (DOL)9 may not scream excitement, but their performance reveals something about how investor preferences are shifting. As the U.S. dollar depreciated10—and as rate expectations turned dovish11—income streams abroad became more attractive. European and Japanese firms, long dismissed, started showing signs of operational resilience. And in a world with AI-driven hype cycles, AIVI stood out by using AI not as a theme to chase, but as a tool to allocate—with algorithms learning where fundamentals were quietly improving, and, in this period, avoiding the classic "traps"12 that have long haunted traditional value funds.

Figure 1: WisdomTree's Top and Bottom Five Performing ETFs in Q1 2025

The Bottom 5 ETFs—Possible Opportunities in the Future

Every quarter has its stars—but it also has its strugglers. And often, those two groups rhyme more than they clash. The five worst-performing WisdomTree ETFs from January to April 2025 aren't just a list of laggards; they're a reminder that the road to transformative opportunity is rarely linear, and is often paved with drawdowns.

Three Thematic Strategies

At the bottom of the list sits the WisdomTree BioRevolution Fund (WDNA).13 Down nearly 13% over the period, it captures a hard truth about biotech and life sciences: progress is exponential, but equity performance is cyclical. The most recent great environment for equity performance among these companies was in 2020 and 2021 when interest rates across developed markets were at or near zero and governments were printing money. When there is macroeconomic volatility, it may not shift the science that these firms are pursuing, but it can make it more difficult for them to raise money. It could also place a higher burden on investors to understand more about the new scientific endeavors the companies are pursuing.

Just ahead of it, the WisdomTree Cloud Computing Fund (WCLD),14 declined by 12.4%. At first glance, this seems counterintuitive. Isn't AI booming? Isn't software "eating the world"15? Yes—but cloud stocks in Q1 faced a valuation reset. Investors are seeking to figure out how much the popular large language models (LLMs) can do without additional software and how many software companies can effectively prove their added value over and above the popular LLMs.

The story of the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Fund (WTAI),16 is more nuanced. WTAI focuses on an expansive interpretation of the AI value chain - semiconductors, enablers, robotics and automation. Technology, broadly speaking, was not a high-performing area of U.S. equity markets in Q1 2025. In short, AI proliferation is proceeding apace—OpenAI noted that they currently have more than 500 million WEEKLY active users17—but the general public market equity performance did not reflect this in Q1 2025.

The two remaining names - the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Growth Fund (QSML)18 and the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund (QGRW)19—tell a broader story about style headwinds. In Q1, quality growth struggled not because the companies were bad, but because the narrative was elsewhere. In particular, we would note that many were looking at the Magnificent 720, prominently featured in QGRW's underlying exposures, and worrying about valuation in recent years. These companies have become notably less expensive of late.

1 The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund seeks total return by investing in gold miners and gold futures contracts.

2 Source: Ines Ferre, "Gold Demand Hits Record Levels as Central Banks Buy at ‘Eye-Watering' Pace." Yahoo!Finance, 2/5/25.

3 Source: Panday et al., "Economic Research: Economic Outlook U.S. Q2 2025: Losing Steam Amid Shifting Policies," S&P Global, 3/25/25.

4 Refers to how the strategy is structured such that $100 invested would lead to notional exposure of $90 to gold miners and $90 to gold futures.

5 CXSE is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned-Enterprises Index.

6 There is a recognition that "technology-focused" equities could be included in such sectors as Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology.

7 DTH is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree International High Dividend Index.

8 AIVI seeks income and capital appreciation by investing primarily in equity securities selected from a universe of developed market equities, excluding the U.S. and Canada, that exhibit value characteristics based on the selection results of a proprietary, quantitative artificial intelligence (AI) model developed by Voya Investment Management Co., LLC.

9 DOL is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree International LargeCap Dividend Index.

10 Source: Jon Sindreu, "Trump's Tariffs Were Supposed to Boost the Dollar. Why the Opposite Happened," Wall Street Journal, 4/3/25.

11 Jed Graham, "Dovish Fed Sees Slow Growth, 'Transitory' Tariff Inflation; S&P 500 Rallies," Investors Business Daily, 3/19/25.

12 Refers to the concept of a "value trap," where a company has an inexpensive valuation but also a very high risk and that valuation keeps getting more and more inexpensive.

13 WDNA is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree BioRevolution Index.

14 WCLD is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the BVP Nasdaq Emerging Cloud Index.

15 Source: Marc Andreesen, "Why Software Is Eating the World," Wall Street Journal, 8/20/11.

16 WTAI is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Index.

17 Source: Hayden Field and Kate Rooney, "OpenAI Closes $40 Billion Funding Round, Largest Private Tech Deal on Record," CNBC, 3/31/25.

18 QSML is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Growth Index.

19 QGRW is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index.

20 Magnificent 7 refers to Amazon.com, Apple, Alphabet, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia and Tesla.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree's London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU's Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor's degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Apple: Forget The Narratives, There's A Bigger Reason Not To Own It Now
data.description:
Investors of all stripes are in danger of taking an outsized risk with the biggest stocks. Their assumption is that when a great company's stock price falls, say by 1/3 of its all-time high price, that's an automatic "buy the dip" situation. That's a lot of what we saw on Wednesday. But take it from a career stock price trend analyst, that alone is not a buy signal.

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has lost several billion dollars in market value, having just bounced up from its worst decline from an all-time high in several years (below). This was due to the uncertainties posed by tariffs, at least in part. But there's much more to the sanguine prospects with this iconic business's stock. The recent plunge in AAPL doesn't open up enough margin of safety to make us optimistic.

Here are 3 reasons to sell/not own Apple stock.

#1 Apple's Valuation Is Not Yet Attractive

Addressing the elephant in the room, yes, Apple is an incredible company that deserves a premium valuation to reward its quality, stability, capital allocation history, and everything else.

But the factor grades below sum it up a bit. In recent months, Apple's valuation has gone from bad to awful.

Even with this rich valuation, its growth also receives a D grade, which makes things even worse. Less tangible factors, such as tariffs and macroeconomic concerns, risk of recessions, and the like, have also worsened in recent months, and even with the drop, Apple's valuation remains stretched.

Its forward price-to-earnings is 25x considering fiscal year 2025, which is still above its historical average.

#2 Apple’s Growth Isn’t Compelling Either

Apple is already a very mature company. Although the smartphone market continues to grow, it is not expected to grow at a double-digit pace over the next few years, actually, according to Statista, the CAGR estimates are closer to 4% for the next few years.

The valuation may not seem so bad, since 25x forward earnings is in line with other tech companies and even better than some like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), but you have to look at it in relation to growth.

Due to these factors, the market estimates a revenue growth for Apple next year of 4.4%, and getting close to the mid-single digit in the coming years. This is quite coherent since services revenue should continue to grow at a faster pace and push revenue upwards, while product revenue, such as iPhone and Mac, should grow at a slower pace on average, more related to price increases and a slight increase in the user base.

Perhaps over the next couple of years, Apple will be able to show good growth numbers due to the iPhone cycle. But overall, this single-digit growth in the top line is not so attractive, especially if we think about the threats to this growth, such as tariffs, macroeconomic uncertainties, strong competition in regions such as China, and regulatory risks that could affect the main pillar of the thesis, services.

#3 Reason: Tariffs Enhance Uncertainty

The third reason is tariffs. To some, it may seem like noise, and really, no one knows exactly how this will play out over the next few years, but in any case, it creates a lot of uncertainty and volatility.

One of the feasible scenarios is that iPhones will become much more expensive, with price hikes of up to $350. And this certainly has the potential to affect the fundamentals of the top and bottom line.

With Apple raising prices to justify tariffs, this should cause demand to fall, especially with the scenario as volatile as it is, many will wait to upgrade their phones. Likewise, by absorbing part of these tariffs, the company also loses margin, i.e., a scenario may emerge in the coming quarters where revenue falls, and EPS falls even further.

In the medium term, the company should be able to resume growth, but there is a high cost to this adaptation. Migrating production, changing contracts, changing logistics, all of this has a high monetary cost in the short term.

Price trend analysis

The daily view here is a good news/bad news situation. It looks better than many stocks. But that's like saying you got a new phone, but it is an iPhone 2. The stock has a prayer of landing once again in the familiar $170 area, where it bottomed or topped 5 times since late 2021. While that is interesting, it is not yet significant.

The bigger issue could be in this very long-term price chart, which informs me that the 20-month moving average is in danger of rolling over. It has been so long that AAPL has been in this predicament, that I'd have to resize the chart to cut out the past 10 years just to find the last time that type of signal was brewing. So hopefully, that makes my point.

Crash…please?

This is a business I'd be thrilled to own if it does a "2013" all over again. I stubbornly waited (since my technical system was not as robust nor responsive as it is now) until the stock, then at single-digit earnings, was selling at a level that in hindsight, would have made my life a lot easier as a portfolio manager and advisor for the next 7 years or so.

I recently owned AAPL in collar form, but the recent meltdown, while limiting my losses quite a bit, only served to reinitiate me to being a hopeful AAPL holder. I think I'll get another chance, but I need more to make a new move here. I will assign a hold rating to AAPL because a sell would imply I'm very pessimistic about it. It is such a big allocation in the S&P 500 index, that it "is" the market to some degree. And I don't like this market, not here, not for now.

data.title: Broadcom: Maintaining Buy Rating On Surprising Upward Earnings Revisions
data.description:
Recently, I have updated my price targets on Apple (AAPL) and Nvidia (NVDA), and what I noted is that the price target adjustments were not so much driven by lower earnings in the years ahead as those revisions to the estimates were less than 2%.

The bigger adjustments to the price targets have come from applying lower EV/EBITDA multiples to account for the added risk in the current trade environment.

I covered Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) in March this year, and since then, the stock has fallen by 11.3%. In this report, I will briefly discuss the risks the companies face, and I will discuss how earnings estimates have changed. I will use those changed estimates and lower trading multiple to establish stock price targets and a rating for the stock.

Broadcom Has Significant Exposure To China

Broadcom is a fabless semiconductor company, and its products are manufactured in the U.S. mostly for its wireless communication equipment and fiber-optic products. Its advanced chips are built in Taiwan.

Technically, the tariffs that China imposed are only applicable to the shipments from the U.S. to China. However, the U.S. could impose even more strict export controls that could limit shipments from Taiwan to China and hurt Broadcom’s sales.

The added complexity and risk for Broadcom is that it does ship goods to China that are used to assemble products that are also destined for the U.S. Those products would be subject to the U.S. tariffs.

This brings the company in the position that it faces a direct impact from the tariffs from its chip products and an indirect impact from chips being implemented in end products. Higher tariffs are likely to erode demand for both, and the higher cost could eventually result in an increase in inflation, which would further erode demand. So, there is a real risk of disposable income and GDP growth declining, which could affect the sales.

Part of the company’s revenues come from the use of its chips in consumer goods and part of it comes from IT modernization projects. When the economy in the U.S. or China tanks, those sales come under pressure. The sales pressure is also not limited to U.S. and China. Due to the size of these economies, it could cause the global economic growth to flatten, which would hurt demand for Broadcom’s product (both semiconductor goods and services) in markets other than the U.S. and China.

According to Broadcom’s annual report (page 88), the company derived $11.53 billion of its sales from China. This is over 22% of the company’s full-year sales. There is no breakdown of sales to China for semiconductor sales and licenses, which are not subject to tariffs, but it is clear that Broadcom has substantial exposure to China. Part of those sales could be subject to tariffs and a potential tightening of export controls.

Previously, I believed that the company had some advantages in the sense that it derives its revenues from goods as well as services with long-term agreements in place. While I still do believe that those elements are a de-risk for Broadcom, the extent of the current trade war even when the U.S. does not impose tariffs on countries other than China brings significant risk to the global economy and puts Broadcom’s business globally at risk regardless of the shielding elements it has in place.

Recession fears have spiked to 79% and that recession fear can affect Broadcom sales with a subsequent hit to sales when a recession actually does happen. So, even if there is no substantial revision of earnings estimates, the risk of those estimates being revised downward in the near future does increase as chances of a recession loom large.

Earnings Estimates Have Increased For Broadcom

Whereas other companies that I covered showed a negative revision to EBITDA and free cash flow, this is not the case for Broadcom. EBITDA estimates have held rather well and are up 0.2% for 2025-2027 while free cash flow estimates are 0.3% higher. This does not mean that there may not be any negative revisions in the future, but it is a stronger sign for Broadcom that could be partially driven by its exposure to services sales.

Broadcom Stock Price Target Lowered

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Broadcom, I am reducing my stock price target from $252.82 to $214.24 implying 24% upside. That is a long-term price target. In the near-term, I do believe there could be downside to $146.84. It is not an extremely favorable risk profile, but based on the downside risk against the company median EV/EBITDA and the upside, I do believe maintaining the buy rating is justified.

Conclusion: Broadcom Remains Attractive

Broadcom definitely does face risk from direct implications of the trade war and indirect implications such as global economic growth tapering. So, it is not the case that Broadcom is free of risk. It has some partial shielding through sales of services which are not subject to tariffs, which obviously is a positive. That does not completely eliminate the risks.

As a result, I am reducing my price target from $252.82 to $214.24, but I do maintain my buy rating for the stock. I remain mindful about the risk to earnings and the uncertainty in the markets, as US tariffs are changing every other day.

data.title: YMAG: I Expect Long-Term Underperformance
data.description:
One of the most popular new investing trends in recent years has been the rise of high yield covered call ETFs. The idea of systemically selling call options on major market indexes such as the S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100 has been well-known for a while. However, ETF providers have launched increasingly diverse products which operate covered call strategies on more niche sets of assets and sectors.

YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG) takes this idea even further. It is a fund of fund which owns seven other YieldMax ETFs which sell covered calls on the so-called magnificent seven stocks. Thus, YMAG serves as a sort of one-stop shop for anyone wanting to sell covered calls on seven mega-cap tech stocks within one product wrapper. It's certainly a novel idea. But is it a good one?

I Believe YMAG Will Underperform Its Underlying Stocks Over Time

In a perfectly rational efficient market, options should be priced correctly, and thus there be no net return to either buyers or sellers of call options on average. In the real world, call options used to be generally overpriced versus underlying volatility, and there was actual alpha to be had be systematically selling call options. However, since this was fact was discovered and institutions began shorting large amounts of option volatility, this opportunity has largely dried up on the major indexes. While we have less long-term data to look at for individual stock volatility selling strategies, I believe a similar effect will play out.

And that's before considering that YMAG has an expense ratio of 1.12% and there can be considerable transaction costs and slippage associated with selling and rolling call options so frequently. All this will make it hard for YMAG to match, let alone exceed the performance of the underlying stocks it is trading unless there is large alpha in the option selling strategy. It's my contention that there is not.

Let me provide some evidence for that assertion. The chart below shows the total returns since inception for two YieldMax single stock option selling funds, which have shown extreme outlier returns.

The first of these is for vaccine maker Moderna (MRNA) whose shares have collapsed over the past year. And the second is for Strategy (MSTR), the Bitcoin-holding company whose shares have soared over the same time period. Both stocks are presented below along with their accompanying YieldMax option income strategy ETF:

As you can see, the YieldMax version underperformed the underlying equity in both cases. For Strategy, its shares are up 282% over this time period, whereas the YieldMax fund is up just 187%, and yes, that includes dividends.

Meanwhile, on the losing end of the spectrum, Moderna stock is down 75% since the YieldMax fund launched, while the YieldMax income version is down 76% including the dividends.

This shows that selling covered calls greatly caps your upside when a stock is moving sharply higher, as Strategy did last year. At the same time, selling covered calls does little to limit losses if a stock goes into a dramatic decline. Even with the dividends paid from the YieldMax MRNA Option Strategy ETF, it has slightly lagged the simpler approach of just owning Moderna stock outright. If you have equal losses in a downturn and smaller gains when the underlying stock goes up, that's not going to be a winning long-term formula.

Is Mag 7 The Best Basket To Sell Options Against?

There's nothing magical about the particular seven stocks that YMAG is selling options against. Over time, the market's winners and losers change. For YieldMax to maintain these seven particular companies at equal allocations, it will have to be continuously rebalancing the fund, cutting position sizes at the winning companies while averaging down on the ones that are losing.

Suppose you ran a fund that owned just the MSTR options income fund and the MRNA options income fund at a 50/50 split. Wouldn't be too bad, right, as the Strategy gains would offset the Moderna losses, right?

Not so fast. Because the fund would need to maintain a 50/50 exposure to the two holdings, it would have been constantly selling down the Strategy position to buy more Moderna over the past year. This doesn't work out well in the end if one of the companies in question goes into terminal decline.

Is it impossible to imagine any of the Magnificent 7 falling on permanent hard times? It's not as likely as Moderna running into trouble, I'll grant you that. But it's not inconceivable that, say, Tesla (TSLA) share could fall 80% from peak to trough at some point, particularly if competition from the likes of Rivian (RIVN) or BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) intensifies. In fact, TSLA stock has already slipped as much as 50% from its highs a quarter ago. Similarly, NVIDIA (NVDA) shares have slumped in recent weeks as analysts raise more doubts about long-term AI demand.

These seven particular companies in the YieldMax fund have been great performers in the past. But there's no guarantee that will continue in the future. And by rebalancing these seven stocks to maintain equal allocations, it creates a likely outcome where the fund will be throwing good money after bad at some point as one or more of these seven companies stops being so magnificent.

YieldMax Mag 7 Fund Historical Returns

How has YMAG performed so far compared to alternatives? Here it is versus the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100:

Granted, the above chart of historical returns since YMAG's launch doesn't look too bad. The fund has outperformed both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 since inception. So, what's the issue?

YMAG has barely outperformed its counterparts, even though it tends to be significantly more volatile when markets go both up and down. YMAG appeared to be significantly outperforming in the recent market rally up through December. But it has lost nearly all that alpha during the recent market correction. I believe YMAG would end up lagging the indexes to a meaningful degree if and when a more prolonged bear market ensues.

Total Returns Do Matter. Don't Be Blinded By Yields

One common argument in favor of YMAG in particular and ultra-high-yielding option strategy ETFs in general is that they give investors income without having to reduce their position sizes. The analogy of not eating one's seed corn is often employed.

And yet, does that logic hold up in practice? I'd argue that it doesn't. Take the aforementioned YieldMax Moderna income fund mentioned above. Here's its price chart:

But you still have all your shares, so there's no problem, right? MRNY stock paid out $5.65/share of dividends over the past 12 months and so as long as the dividends keep on flowing in, things should be okay.

Unfortunately for shareholders, that's not how it works out. Because the fund's assets are sharply declining, the amount of covered call income it can generate from selling options also declines sharply.

Just stop and consider the chart of MRNY's dividend payouts since January 2024:

In early 2024, MRNY was paying close to $1/share per month in dividends. Fast-forward a year, this has declined to less than 20 cents a share per month, and the figure continues to decrease as time marches onward. This is the textbook definition of a melting ice cube.

The case of Moderna is a particularly stark one, since that company seems like it was something of a one-hit wonder tied to the COVID-19 vaccine moment. Once that passed, so did the company's general business prospects.

However, it's informative to the extent of how many covered call funds work in general, albeit usually on a slower timeframe. Over time, the assets in a fund will be eroded. When a stock goes up, the fund's gains are capped. But when the stock drops, the fund's NAV takes a hit. The market over the past week is a perfect example of this, with huge rallies and massive declines that far exceeded the levels of volatility that option sellers were expecting. It's almost inevitable that the net asset value of such a structure is going to trend downward over time, and as NAV declines, so does the inherent income potential remaining at the fund.

People may argue that owning a product like YMAG allows them to have their cake and eat it too. But the income comes at the cost of erosion of one's invested capital.

Covered call sellers earn their premiums because there are options buyers that believe that they are going to make a profit buying those derivative instruments. And, over time, the options market usually more or less equals out between buyers and sellers. Sometimes one side or the other has a small house edge, but it tends to not be either permanent or especially dramatic.

Because option selling doesn't have a particularly large inherent edge (and is sometimes an outright losing strategy), this isn't income in the traditional sense. In practice, it feels a lot more like return of capital, where your initial investment is slowly being given back to you. There's nothing wrong with that. There are other financial assets, such as annuities, that are structured along similar grounds. But please be aware of the mechanics of how these sorts of covered call funds work. This is not a sustainable long-term dividend income stream of the sort you'd get from a company that has rising earnings and thus can pay rising dividends over time.

YMAG Verdict

It's my view that this fund of ETFs structure exposes investors to fairly high fees while maintaining an equal-weighted basket of stock exposures that may not be ideal over the long term.

YMAG is showing a particularly high quoted yield at the moment. But this is tied to the unusually high levels of implied volatility in the stock market as well. When the present volatility spike ends, so will the unusually large dividend payments from YMAG. Meanwhile, the recent market gyrations are likely to significantly erode the underlying net asset values of the funds in which YMAG invests.

I'm not necessarily against covered calls as an options' strategy. But I don't see this fund as being a particularly attractive way to get exposure to that theme.

data.title: WeRide And The Explosive Growth Of The RoboTaxi Industry
data.description:
The robotaxi market could have more potential for share price appreciation than any other industry. Industry analysts forecast extraordinary growth over the next 5 years, and the market is concentrated with only 7 or 8 players. This could be an actual millionaire-maker market.

I am keen to get a piece of this market and will look at each player in detail this month. I am starting with WeRide (NASDAQ:WRD), they have made significant progress in recent years and is one of the two pure-play robotaxi companies.

Market Forecasts

The forecasts for the growth of this industry are extraordinary. Every forecasting company I use has the most incredible growth rates built in.

It represents an opportunity on a scale that is unmatched at present. If these forecasts are even remotely accurate, the players in this market have hundred-bagger potential.

ARK Invest Big Ideas 2025

I read the ARK Invest Big Ideas publication each year.

This year, there is a large section on RoboTaxis. It gives two essential figures: firstly, they estimate the 2030 RoboTaxi market at $10 trillion, and that the industry will generate $34 trillion in equity value, with the majority of it going to the robotaxi platform providers.

ARK believes a collapse in the cost of taxi rides will drive the industry's growth. They forecast a fall from $2 per mile to $0.25 per mile.

The cost per mile of a RoboTaxi vs. current ride-hailing taxi rides

The $0.25 per mile seems outrageous. However, the current cost of a taxi in China is around $0.50 per mile, I got that using an app to book a taxi. (DiDi in Shanghai).

The Main Players

The industry currently has eight major players, two of which are private AutoX and May Mobility. AutoX operates around 1,000 taxis.

Two other players are yet to reach commercial operations: Tesla has not yet announced its plans, but has a widely publicized robotaxi day due soon. Amazon (AMZN), through its Zoox subsidiary, is still testing but will begin commercial operations in Las Vegas soon.

Motional is one further player working with Hyundai, but we have limited information about their plans. Motional is the descendant of nuTonomy, the first RoboTaxi company.

That leaves four commercial robotaxi companies we can invest in that I will concentrate on: WeRide, Pony (PONY), Waymo (GOOG), and Baidu (BIDU).

Three elephants in this room

Three huge ride-hailing companies can be expected to make significant progress in this market. UBER Technologies (UBER), Didi Global (OTCPK:DIDIY) and Lyft.

UBER is the world's biggest taxi company, operating in around 70 countries and 10,000 cities.

UBER may have decided to be the platform on which people book their Robotaxi ride, rather than developing a Robotaxi. They have partnered with Waymo in Austin, Texas, and plan to expand the partnership.

UBER has partnered with WeRide to bring robotaxis to Dubai; the authorities hope to have 25% of all taxis autonomous before 2030.

Didi is a global platform-based mobility provider. It operates in China, Brazil, and Mexico, amongst others. Didi has more than 550 million users and tens of millions of drivers. In 2016 Didi acquired UBER China.

Lyft has partnered with May Mobility to begin a robotaxi service in Atlanta in 2025.

Platform Operators and Taxi Manufacturing

ARK Invest believes the biggest gainers will be the RoboTaxi platform operators, rather than the companies selling the Taxis. If they are right about the $15,000 price, they will also be right about the platform operators being the biggest winners.

When I review each company, I will consider its business model, the potential of its ride-hailing platform, and its physical taxis. I will cover DiDi and Uber separately, but as the largest ride-hailing platforms, they might have the most to gain.

Countries and Cities

Robotaxis would seem to have a competitive advantage in densely populated cities with a shortage of Taxi drivers. The large cities in China offer high usage rates, Japan and Singapore have costly taxi fares driven by the shortage of workers, and the highly dense cities of Europe have significant shortages of drivers in all forms of transport.

WeRide is the first company to make progress in several countries and cities. They operate 1,000 vehicles in over 30 cities across ten countries (20F p66). They have commercial operations in China, France, UAE and Singapore. Interestingly, they state, "the U.S. has not been, and will not be, a market for our products or services" (20F p73). Perhaps that gives them Tariff immunity.

The geographical spread may become a key advantage, allowing them to scale their business faster than the competition.

Autonomous Vehicles

This article is focused on Robotaxis, but the technology of autonomous vehicles is suitable for multiple other use cases. Both Pony.AI and WeRide are trying to exploit this; Pony.AI has developed an autonomous trucking division, and WeRide has three types of autonomous vehicles, all developing commercial traction.

WeRide believes it can get these other vehicles certified before taxis, making the certification process far easier for their technology.

RoboBus, RoboSweeper, RoboTaxi and RoboVans

Total sales are very small, 91 vehicles in 2024 is a drop in the ocean in taxi sales. New York has over 63,000 taxis, again suggesting the possibility of near-exponential growth.

RoboBus has been deployed in eight countries: Switzerland, France, Japan, Singapore, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, and China. The vehicle has a top speed of 40km/hr and can operate on the open road but generally follows a pre-defined route, often used to ferry customers to airports or to and from large stadiums.

JMC-Ford builds RoboVan; WeRide has received over 10,000 orders for RoboVan (20F P69). The vehicle can be used for point-to-point distribution and addresses many of the problems of driver shortages. It is a low-cost ownership solution to a growing problem.

RoboSweeper is manufactured by Yutong (a related party that also makes the RoboBus (20F p134) and Ecar Tech. The sweeper comes in two sizes, the S6 is the larger and is designed for standard road washing and sweeping. The smaller S1 is designed to navigate around small obstacles. Orders for WeRide S1 have exceeded several million dollars.

WeRide has almost 2,000 days of commercial robotaxi operations without a single safety incident. WeRide's newest GXR model began commercial production in late 2024 and is expected to scale in 2025. Nissan, GAC, and GEELY are the OEMs involved in the project. The vehicle features a new, cheaper sensor suite and a revamped high-performance computing platform.

The WeRide Business Model

The chosen business model of each player in this market will be a key differentiating factor in my decision to invest. In the case of WeRide, the business model has two strands.

International Business

In the international arena (outside of China), WeRide will generate revenue from selling taxis to operators and ongoing service and technical support income. They intend to focus on collaborating with leading platforms to ensure higher utilization levels.

The first success of this strategy was the agreement with UBER to launch robotaxi services in Abu Dhabi in December 2024. The goal is to have a fleet of 50 taxis by the end of 2025.

WeRide has guided test operations in Switzerland this year and has autonomous permits already granted in 4 countries.

WeRide believes it can open new markets quicker than the competition because of the RoboSweeper and RoboBus products.

From Q4 Q and A

Most authorities, like municipal governments or state governments, will prefer a responsible approach when they adopt AV. And WeRide has a way to gradually introduce automobile driving products, like we can always introduce RoboSweeper at first, and also fixed-route minibus, which the challenges for safety for this product relatively low compared to the requirements for robotaxi.

China Domestic Market

China WeRide will run its taxi-hailing platform, WeRideGo, and compete against BIDU, PONY, and DiDi in this market. Currently, the revenue from WeRideGo is insignificant (P101 20F). Still, they have established subsidiary companies to run the taxi network in various cities, including Guangzhou, Shenzhen, Wuhan, Nanjing, Beijing, Shanghai, Zhengzhou, Wuxi, Xi'an, Anqing, and Chongqing.

Bosch

In 2022 WeRide entered a partnership with Bosch to act as a tier 2 supplier providing R and D services. The partnership included an investment by Bosch and is expected to focus on delivering L2 and 3 ADS in the Chinese market.

Technology

All of the competitors in this race, apart from Tesla, are using a similar range of hardware, integrating cameras, LiDAR, and radar. (Tesla remains Camera only)

The real area of competition is the software, the AI, and machine learning algorithms that enable these vehicles to travel safely on busy inner-city streets. It is not an easy task. We know that GM Cruise withdrew from the market, and no large auto OEM other than Tesla has concrete plans to enter this market. (ARK Big Ideas)

ARK believes Tesla has an enormous advantage over the field because of the miles its vehicles have traveled and the amount of data generated, in AI data counts.

WeRide is not featured in the Big Ideas 2025, but they have driven for 40 million KM without a single incident. On P81, ARK says that Waymo has surpassed 400,000 miles per incident and that Tesla hopes to achieve 10,000 miles in FSD and 700,000 miles on its robotaxis. WeRide is running at almost 25 million miles, far ahead of its two US competitors.

WeRide believes that data recorded from human drivers will limit the performance of robotaxis trained on that data to human performance. That will not be good enough to get the reduced insurance costs and regulatory acceptance they aim for.

WeRide uses a hybrid approach to its AI. It has an end-to-end model (where one neural network controls all sensing and mapping, rather than discrete systems). The WeRide approach includes maps. The cars can develop maps when off-road or in unmapped areas. WeRide outsources HD mapping to affiliated companies.

WeRide uses the 40 million km of driving data but supplements it with extensive simulated data to develop multiple long-tail examples that a car may never encounter. For instance, few vehicles will have seen a light aircraft landing on a highway, and probably no Teslas, Waymos, or WeRides will have seen it. Still, it has happened, and WeRide has developed extensive simulated data for many potential long-tail scenarios. The goal is to make the autonomous car a far better driver than a human.

WeRide Finances

WeRide has a strong balance sheet: $950 million of shareholder equity and a total debt of $10 million.

WeRide has a three-year cash runway, but net income is heading lower as costs increase. 2025 and 2026 appear to be more years of testing with a small but growing fleet of autonomous vehicles.

Net income was nearly -3 billion RMB in the last 12 months (-$400 million), revenue has remained relatively flat since 2022, and the company has never created positive cash flow.

A summary of the income statement shows significant and growing R&D and a ballooning SG&A figure. In Q4 2024, total operating expenses increased 82%.

In the 20-F (P25) WeRide wrote, "To continue to support our growth and strategic objectives, we need significant capital"

And on page 26 said

We plan to raise capital in the near term, through equity or other financings including issuance of additional shares or ADSs to achieve the foregoing and other strategic goals.

It is too early in the commercialization process for this company to make predictions about revenue or profits.

The UBER deal in Dubai only predicts 50 taxis by mid-2025. Adding at that rate will do little to kickstart WeRide's revenue growth.

Revenue for 2024 was $49 million, delivering a gross profit of $15 million. Total operating expenses were $313 million. WeRide has a long way to go to profitability, but things could change quickly. R&D costs will collapse when the tech is complete, and revenue may soar in triple digits when the robotaxi services are running.

Wall Street is forecasting revenue growth in 2025 of 60% and a breakeven date of 2028/9. That growth rate is low compared to the forecast of this industry's exponential growth rates.

Conclusion

The robotaxi market looks set for significant growth; it has a limited number of players, and the technology required establishes high barriers of entry.

WeRide is one of only two pure-play robotaxi companies we can invest in, but the pure-play companies face the might of Amazon, Alphabet and Tesla. The pure plays will have to move quickly to establish operations and a safety record, gain regulatory acceptance in areas likely to adopt robotaxis, and provide high utilization rates.

WeRide is progressing well on these fronts; it has operations in several large Chinese cities and plans to expand. It is in partnership with UBER to develop a robotaxi service in Abu Dhabi. WeRide is authorized in multiple countries and is testing in several new ones.

The business model outside of China is to sell autonomous vehicles and services; inside China, they will operate their own ride-hailing platform.

On balance, I have decided not to invest yet. I want to get exposure to this industry, but WeRide seems full of risk and uncertainty.

WeRide will need substantial funds in the coming years; they currently have minimal sales, and the ramp-up this year looks small, and regulatory acceptance may continue to move slowly. They have better-funded competitors, and the WeRideGo app is still small; likely, they will need to develop good partnerships with the major ride-hailing platforms to make sufficient progress.

On the positive side, once they do get regulation in place and access to the big ridesharing apps, WeRide has the technology, the products, and the manufacturing partners to scale quickly into a significant business.

I will review this company regularly and report on the other players in the market this month, hoping to find a solid investment case.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: VICI Properties, General Dynamics, LandBridge, Anavex +
data.description:
Undercovered Dozen Introduction

The 'Undercovered' Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between April 4 – April 10.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Buy Alert: Trinity Capital Now Offers A 14% Covered Yield

On The Pulse | Buy

Crashing stock prices amid an increase in market volatility have opened up a new buying window for business development company Trinity Capital Inc. (NASDAQ:TRIN).

The stock price of Trinity Capital plunged 7.38% on Friday, causing the business development company’s yield to skyrocket past 14%. That said, though, the BDC covers its dividend with net investment income and has possibly a shot of growing its dividend as well in 2025.

I think that the market crash last week is an excellent opportunity to build positions in a high-quality, leading business development company of Trinity Capital’s caliber.

In my last piece on Trinity Capital, I underscored what I perceived to be the core pillars of the underlying investment levers, which were:

  1. Positive interest rate sensitivity;

  2. A robust dividend; and

  3. The offering of specialized financing solutions, particularly in the equipment market, which separated the BDC from other business development companies.

Moreover, Trinity Capital’s positive trend in total investment income as well as a much more affordable valuation on the dip are two more reasons why Trinity Capital remains my favorite BDC to buy.

VICI Properties: Virtually Tariff-Proof And Growing Like A Weed

PropNotes | Buy

In this article, we wanted to talk more about VICI Properties Inc. (VICI), another key commercial REIT with a portfolio focused on gaming and experiential real estate. While VICI's growth trajectory isn't quite as glamorous as some of the more popular tech firms, the firm's rock-solid tenants, strong liquidity, and domestically rooted business model should protect investors from increasing global tensions.

Today, we'll outline VICI's prospects, explain why the REIT is virtually 'Tariff Proof', and detail why we see the stock as a 'Buy' in the current market environment.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

As we've never publicly written about VICI before here on Seeking Alpha, let's begin by taking a look at the company's business.

In short, VICI owns a multi-billion-dollar portfolio of real estate that it uses to generate rental revenues. From there, the company uses cash flows, debt, and equity financing to build the portfolio while also growing payouts to investors.

The business model is simple, but proven.

Caterpillar Vs. Deere: Which One Is The Better Buy Amidst Tariffs?

Luca Socci | Buy

This article presents a comparative analysis of two iconic industrial companies in America: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) and Deere & Company (DE).

Perhaps, some years from now, April 2 will be a date in history textbooks. Indeed, it will probably end up in economic history. Right now, however, we are busy trying first to figure out how the announced tariffs work and, more importantly, what impact they may have on the economy. Jerome Powell, for example, expects the impact of tariffs to be "larger than expected". The outcome of higher inflation and slower growth, said Powell, seems likely. Undoubtedly, industrials represent a sector that has concerned investors. Yes, a manufacturing renaissance may be due, but a manufacturing recession (or at least a crisis) seems the short-term outcome.

So, how can we compare Caterpillar and Deere, trying to reflect the current uncertainties regarding their outlook? Well, I will use a few parameters to build a scoring model for the two.

LandBridge: A High-Quality Long-Term Investment Choice

Symeon Mavroudis | Buy

LandBridge Company LLC (LB) is one of my favorite investment picks. Below, I will outline the reasons why I am issuing a “BUY” recommendation for this truly remarkable stock.

Its unique business model, strong financials, and most importantly its robust upward momentum support my view.

This is for sure a stock better suited for medium to long-term investors who are not afraid of short-term volatility, but they prefer to see dips as opportunities to strengthen their positions.

While LandBridge’s current dividend policy is minimal, I believe this is likely to change, aligning with and potentially driving greater investor demand.

Let’s start with the performance of the stock. LandBridge was listed on NYSE in late June 2024 at an IPO price of $17 per share (14.5 million shares plus an additional 2.175 million allocated to underwriters through the 30-day option). Since then, it has gained over +290% despite the recent market pressure following the announcement of the new U.S. tariff policy.

The quality of the stock’s rally stands as a more critical factor than its actual return value. The small size of pullbacks indicates that shareholders maintain strong support and genuine buying interest in the overall uptrend.

The Company fundamentals which I will analyze below together with its current resilience strengthen my investment conviction for its long-term value. The upcoming years will likely produce additional growth that makes any negative outcome seem remote.

ONEOK: A Unique Buying Opportunity

The Asian Investor | Strong Buy

ONEOK, Inc. (OKE) is a leading midstream platform with an increasing pipeline network that allows the company to deliver highly predictable and stable distributable cash flow to the company’s shareholders. ONEOK is delivering strong DCF, EBITDA, and dividend growth and has been focused on stemming major acquisitions in order to boost its business and diversify its portfolio. I expect ONEOK to announce more pipeline acquisitions going forward and with the midstream company also out-performing other midstream plays in terms of distribution growth, I believe ONEOK is one of the most attractive companies currently on sale, in my opinion.

My last recommendation for investors to buy the drop in the shares of ONEOK was published in December 2024: A Solid Midstream Play. However, the current market meltdown, driven by concerns about U.S. tariffs and their impact on trade, has created a unique situation in which investors can buy high-quality midstream plays (like ONEOK) at a deeply discounted price to the historical valuation. I believe the risk profile is highly attractive, especially because ONEOK is achieving a high percentage of its recurring earnings from fee-based delivery contracts, and the midstream company is outperforming other midstream companies (although not everyone) in terms of dividend growth.

Defense Is The Tariff Safe Play - Initiating General Dynamics With A Buy

The Techie | Buy

General Dynamics Corporation (GD) does just about everything regarding defense. If you need a custom jet built, a tank, or even a nuclear submarine, you can bet General Dynamics can deliver. You’d have to go back to 1977 to find out when they went public, trading at just 35 cents a share; it seems they've been around forever. They split their products across four main areas: aerospace, combat, marine, and tech. All of these work on contract-based orders; governments with billions to spend will place orders for the likes of planes, boats, and surveillance systems, and General Dynamics will go about making them. They currently boast a backlog of over $90 billion. To put this in context, their total revenue last year was $47.7 billion, so clearly, governments are buying. Based in Reston, Virginia, they’re a homegrown US company and a particular favorite of President Donald Trump, they’re the 4th largest acceptor of contracts with the Department of Defence. With Trump in office for the next 4 years at least, I believe General Dynamics' order backlog will keep growing, following the same trend as US military spending channeled to the OG players. For the fiscal year 2025, the government has outlined a total of $682 billion in spending obligations so far. While there's a lot of news coming out from Pete Hegseth about an 8% cut in defense spend over the next five years that has the market in panic, I think these guys will be safer than most. I'm gonna break down General Dynamics’ business model to give you a closer look at why I think this stock has more upside and is a buy in my book.

OBDC: Sell-Off Makes Its Yield Soar To 13%

Jonathan Weber | Buy

Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) is a solid Business Development Company that has recently seen its shares slump as part of a broad market downturn. This has made its dividend yield jump to a very nice level of 13%, while its valuation has come down significantly. While we do not yet know how long this market downturn will last, Blue Owl Capital looks attractive for income investors right here.

I have written about Blue Owl Capital Corporation in the past here on Seeking Alpha once, in November 2024. I gave Blue Owl Capital a "Buy" rating back then due to factors such as the merger with its sister company Blue Owl Capital Corporation III, with OBDC's shares rising a little through February, before shares pulled back during the current market rout. Still, Blue Owl Capital has outperformed the market since my last article, thanks to its generous dividends, with the total return standing at a negative 6% versus a 16% slump for the S&P 500 over the same time frame. With Blue Owl Capital Corporation having reported its most recent earnings results since my last article, and with both the valuation and the dividend yield changing, it is time for an update.

Business Development Companies are usually very US-focused, and as lenders, they don't depend on the export or import of goods to any material degree. Some of their portfolio companies may be impacted by tariffs and trade worries, so there is a potential indirect impact on Business Development Companies from the current trade war or trade tensions, although that is not necessarily a problem for BDC and other lenders -- even if one of OBDC's portfolio companies becomes less profitable due to tariffs, e.g. if it is a retailer that imports goods made overseas, that is only a problem for OBDC if this portfolio company is unable to pay interest to OBDC. If a portfolio company is impacted by tariffs and becomes less profitable without running into deeper financial problems, the company will continue to pay interest to its lenders, such as OBDC, and OBDC will not feel a negative impact. Until we see the company's Q2 results, where the impact of the new tariffs might show up, we do not know what the actual impact on OBDC will look like. There is potential for headwinds due to possibly higher defaults and write-offs, but to me, it seems unlikely that Blue Owl Capital Corporation and other diversified lenders will be hard-hit -- they do not look like the most exposed companies to the new tariff and trade measures at all.

The Other Five Fit For Mention

B2Gold Is One Of The Cheapest Gold Mining Companies With A Near Term Catalyst

Alpha Mantra | Buy

The surge in gold prices in recent quarters has left many investors in awe. Many investors are now looking into gold mining companies that often have an operational leverage effect on their bottom line with rising gold prices. One of the gold miners is B2Gold Corp. (BTG). Among its gold mining peers, BTG has been one of the worst-performing stocks in the last 12 months and is among the cheapest of the lot. However, I believe this should change in 2025 with a ramp-up in production as well as its low valuation. I believe the company has a decent upside from the current price point. Moreover, surging gold prices should further benefit the company.

It's Raining Income: PDO Yields 12.5%

Rida Morwa | Buy

U.S. Treasuries are debt, so when interest rates are going higher, that means the prices of treasuries are declining. The prices of most debt tend to follow. When interest rates are flat or declining, the prices of debt investments tend to rally since borrowers ultimately owe the par value of the debt, not necessarily the price an investor paid for it.

When it comes to debt funds, PIMCO has long been one of our favorite managers. For decades, PIMCO has demonstrated an ability to navigate the debt markets. With PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO), PIMCO is taking an approach that is very similar to its older funds like PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY). We've covered both before, and it has outperformed the market since our last coverage.

The Ideal Anti-Tariff Investment: TJX Companies

Mycroft Friedrich | Hold

The TJX Companies, Inc. (TJX) is one of the best retail operations I have ever analyzed. Many of you may have shopped at its stores, TJ Maxx, Marshall’s, or HomeGoods, and did not know the parent company was TJX Companies. I am both a Quantitative and Qualitative Analyst, which means that I analyze management operations as well as financials. It is my belief that the current management of TJX is elite and runs a very unique business model, which I have not seen duplicated anywhere else.

What TJX Companies does is send out 1,000s of professional buyers to manufacturers and have them buy overstock or discontinued merchandise, but these buyers are also instructed to only buy higher quality merchandise rather than liquidation merchandise like Dollar Stores do, for example. For those who have shopped in a Marshall's, T.J. Maxx or HomeGoods stores know, you will only find name brands sold there at about 50% off suggested retail prices. Therefore, a shopper in its stores keeps going back over and over again as they know that they will get high quality at a great price.

MARA Holdings: Buy As The NAV Of Its Bitcoin Holdings And Market Cap Converge

Edward Vranic, CFA | Buy

MARA Holdings, Inc. (MARA) is a well-known bet on Bitcoin. The bulk of its assets are in the form of Bitcoin, while the rest of the company's value is in its Bitcoin mining operations. The bearish argument on MARA is a macroeconomic one. If the price of Bitcoin declines, so will MARA. That might be true, but if one is bearish on Bitcoin, there are numerous options out there to place a bearish bet on it rather than MARA. From a fundamental perspective, MARA is a buy and if one is worried about the price of Bitcoin, that risk can always be hedged by opening a bearish position on a Bitcoin ETF.

MARA and the price of Bitcoin are naturally quite highly correlated. At the start of 2025, MARA began to underperform Bitcoin, while the recent market volatility has accelerated that trend. While Bitcoin is up 13% over the past 52 weeks, MARA has declined by nearly 40%.

Anavex: Understanding Their Alzheimer's Open-Label Extension Data Skeptically

C. C. Abbott | Sell

On Saturday, April 5, Anavex Life Sciences Corp. (AVXL), a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company, announced data from their open-label extension (OLE) trial in early Alzheimer's Disease (AD), which they presented at the "AD/PDTM 2025 Conference".

ATTENTION-AD is a follow-on trial from a previously completed double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled phase 2b/3 AD trial.

I take a close look at ATTENTION-AD results and share my thoughts here.

data.title: TXNM Energy: Profit From Positive Demographics And Data Center Expansion
data.description:
TXNM Energy, Inc. (NYSE:TXNM) is a regulated electric utility that serves approximately 825,000 customers located throughout Texas and New Mexico:

The company that is currently known as TXNM Energy was formerly known as PNM Resources. Long-time readers might remember that we have discussed PNM Resources in various previous articles a few years ago. At the time, the management of PNM Resources was pushing hard for a merger with Iberdrola’s (OTCPK:IBDSF) Avangrid, which operates in the Mid-Atlantic and New England regions, but regulators were largely opposed to that deal. After much deliberation and back and forth with regulators, the deal was scuttled early last year. As such, it appears that a merger is no longer a possibility for this company, and investors should simply evaluate it as a standalone electric utility operating in New Mexico and Texas.

We can see, however, that the utility operation in New Mexico is vastly larger than the one in Texas. While TXNM does have a presence in some of the most populated areas of Texas (notably the suburbs of Dallas and Houston), it only has approximately 275,000 customers in that state. The company’s customer base in New Mexico is roughly twice the size at 550,000 customers. This comes from the company’s dominance in three of the largest cities in New Mexico – Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, and Albuquerque. As such, New Mexico is somewhat more important for the future of the company, but both states offer growth opportunities for the company and indeed, TXNM Energy appears to be positioned as one of the most rapidly growing utilities that we have discussed in this column. As such, investors interested in both safety and growth may find a lot to like here.

Recent Performance Analysis

We last discussed TXNM Energy on June 14, 2023, when it was still known as PNM Resources. The utility sector (IDU) since that time has been rather volatile due mostly to changing expectations regarding interest rates. Utilities were one of the worst-performing sectors in 2023, and they substantially underperformed the market (SP500):

This disappointing performance reversed itself in 2024, as easing monetary policies resulted in a rebound in these traditionally high-yielding companies. The utility sector gained an impressive 19.07% in 2024, which underperformed the market, but was still overall a better showing than what these companies delivered in the previous year:

As such, we might expect TXNM Energy to have delivered a somewhat mixed performance since our last discussion. The fact that it was forced to abandon the merger with Avangrid could be another blow to its stock performance over the intervening period, but the abandonment of the merger was really no surprise to most investors who followed the company, so it was likely already priced into the stock. This is even more evident when we consider that the company’s stock price did not decline significantly following the announcement that the merger had been abandoned.

Overall, TXNM is up a meager 8.14% since our last discussion, significantly worse than the 20.48% gain that the S&P 500 Index delivered over the same period:

The company’s stock also underperformed if we look at it on a total return basis:

We can clearly see underperformance against both the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) and the U.S. Utilities Index, so there is certainly nothing impressive here. The fact that the company’s stock has underperformed may represent a buying opportunity, though, so we should investigate further.

About TXNM Energy

As stated in the introduction, TXNM Energy is a regulated electric and natural gas company that serves approximately 825,000 customers in the states of Texas and New Mexico. Of the two, New Mexico is the more important state, as it is home to about two-thirds of the company’s customers.

The common perception of New Mexico in particular is that the state is a vast desert with a very low population. There is some truth to this, as New Mexico is one of the least densely populated states in the United States. The U.S. Census Bureau estimates that the state currently has a population of 2,130,256 people spread out over 121,590 square miles. This would give the state an estimated population density of 17.52 people per square mile, lower than all except for five states (South Dakota, North Dakota, Montana, Wyoming, and Alaska). However, a simple look at the population density does not tell the whole story, since the vast majority of the population of New Mexico lives in one of a handful of large cities. According to the World Population Review, approximately 557,198 people, or roughly one-fourth of the state’s population, live in Albuquerque. TXNM Energy is the primary provider of electricity to Albuquerque, so this alone gives the company a dominant presence in the state. In addition, another estimated 90,125 call the city of Santa Fe home. These two cities thus have a combined estimated population of 647,323 people, which is almost one-third of the entire state’s population.

As we can see in the map above, TXNM Energy serves both of these cities and the area between them. The area between them includes the city of Rio Rancho, which has a current population of 114,800 people. If we add this number to the previous figure, we get a population of 762,123 people, which is 35.78% of the state’s estimated population. This basically results in the company having a much larger customer base than we might expect from a utility that is operating in New Mexico.

TXNM Energy is thus a larger utility than might be expected. At the current stock price, the company boasts a market capitalization of $4.64 billion and an enterprise value of $10.52 billion. Here is how that compares to a few other comparable utilities:

Company Name

Market Capitalization

Enterprise Value

TXNM Energy

$4.64 billion

$10.52 billion

NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE)

$6.51 billion

Avista Corporation (AVA)

$3.22 billion

$6.34 billion

Otter Tail Corporation (OTTR)
